era_context: The mid-to-late 1870s sat at the intersection of European imperial expansion, post-Civil War Reconstruction in the United States, the consolidation of Meiji Japan, and the accelerating decline of the Ottoman Empire. The Long Depression (1873–1879) suppressed global trade and fuelled social unrest across industrialised nations. European powers were positioning themselves for the Scramble for Africa, while in East Asia, Qing China and Joseon Korea faced intensifying pressure from both Western powers and a modernising Japan. This was a period of rapid technological innovation set against deepening colonial violence and environmental catastrophe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Southeast Asia (Siam, Burma, Vietnam have court records but are underrepresented in English-language training data)
Central Asia (dominated by Russian imperial narratives)

events.title: Alexander Graham Bell patents the telephone
events.description: On 14 February 1876, Alexander Graham Bell filed a patent application for an 'improvement in telegraphy' — the telephone — at the U.S. Patent Office, receiving Patent No. 174,465 on 7 March 1876. Famously, Elisha Gray filed a caveat for a similar device on the same day as Bell's application, triggering one of the most contested patent disputes in American history. Bell demonstrated the device on 10 March 1876, transmitting the first intelligible speech ('Mr. Watson, come here — I want to see you'). The invention would reshape global communications within a generation, though its practical deployment was still years away.
key_figures: Gardiner Greene Hubbard (Bell's patron and future father-in-law)

sources.name: U.S. Patent No. 174,465 (7 March 1876)
Bell's laboratory notebooks, Library of Congress
Robert V. Bruce, Bell: Alexander Graham Bell and the Conquest of Solitude (1973)

events.certainty_note: Patent records, laboratory notebooks, and extensive contemporary press coverage establish precise dates. The priority dispute with Gray has been exhaustively litigated and studied.

events.title: Battle of the Little Bighorn — Custer's Last Stand
events.description: On 25–26 June 1876, a coalition of Lakota Sioux, Northern Cheyenne, and Arapaho warriors led by Sitting Bull and Crazy Horse annihilated five companies of the U.S. 7th Cavalry under Lieutenant Colonel George Armstrong Custer at the Little Bighorn River. Approximately 268 U.S. soldiers were killed, making it the worst U.S. Army defeat of the Great Sioux War of 1876. The battle was a decisive tactical victory for the Plains nations but provoked a massive U.S. military escalation that ultimately destroyed the remaining independence of the northern Plains peoples within two years. The event became one of the most mythologised episodes in American history.
key_figures: George Armstrong Custer (Lt. Col., 7th Cavalry — killed)
Alfred Terry (Brig. Gen., commanding Dakota Column)

sources.name: Official U.S. Army after-action reports (Reno Court of Inquiry, 1879)
Lakota and Cheyenne oral testimony collected by Walter Camp and others (early 20th century)
Nathaniel Philbrick, The Last Stand: Custer, Sitting Bull, and the Battle of the Little Bighorn (2010)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented from both U.S. military and Native oral sources. Archaeological work at the battlefield (especially the 1983–1985 excavations) has clarified tactical details.

events.title: Bulgarian April Uprising against Ottoman rule
events.description: In April–May 1876, Bulgarian revolutionaries launched an insurrection against Ottoman rule, centred in the towns of Koprivshtitsa, Panagyurishte, and Batak. The uprising was poorly coordinated and quickly suppressed by Ottoman irregular troops (bashi-bazouks), who perpetrated widespread massacres of civilian populations. Estimates of Bulgarian deaths range from 15,000 to 30,000. The atrocities — publicised in Western Europe by journalist Januarius MacGahan and condemned by William Gladstone in his pamphlet 'The Bulgarian Horrors and the Question of the East' — shifted European public opinion decisively against the Ottoman Empire and contributed directly to the Russo-Turkish War of 1877–1878.
key_figures: William Gladstone (British politician, published condemning pamphlet)

sources.name: Januarius MacGahan, reports in the London Daily News (1876)
William Gladstone, The Bulgarian Horrors and the Question of the East (1876)
R.J. Crampton, Bulgaria (2007)

events.certainty_note: Documented by contemporary Western journalists, diplomatic correspondence, and Ottoman records. Casualty figures remain debated, but the event itself is firmly attested.

events.title: Serbia and Montenegro declare war on the Ottoman Empire
events.description: On 30 June 1876, Serbia and Montenegro declared war on the Ottoman Empire, ostensibly in support of Slavic populations in Bosnia-Herzegovina who had revolted in 1875. The Serbian army, bolstered by Russian volunteers including General Mikhail Chernyayev, suffered repeated defeats and was saved from destruction only by Russian diplomatic intervention forcing an armistice in November. The war exposed Ottoman military resilience but also deepened Russian commitment to intervening directly, setting the stage for the Russo-Turkish War of 1877–1878. Montenegro fared better, gaining territory in several engagements.
key_figures: Prince Milan Obrenović IV (ruler of Serbia)

sources.name: British diplomatic correspondence, Foreign Office archives (1876)
Barbara Jelavich, History of the Balkans: Eighteenth and Nineteenth Centuries (1983)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in European diplomatic archives, press coverage, and military records.

events.title: Ottoman constitutional crisis — depositions of Abdülaziz and Murad V, accession of Abdul Hamid II
events.description: 1876 saw extraordinary political upheaval in the Ottoman Empire. On 30 May, Sultan Abdülaziz was deposed in a palace coup led by reformist ministers Midhat Pasha and Hüseyin Avni Pasha; he died days later, officially by suicide. His nephew Murad V, who succeeded him, suffered a mental breakdown and was in turn deposed on 31 August after only 93 days. Abdul Hamid II then acceded to the throne, promising constitutional government. On 23 December 1876, Abdul Hamid promulgated the first Ottoman constitution (Kanun-i Esasi), drafted largely by Midhat Pasha, establishing a parliament. Abdul Hamid would suspend this constitution within two years and rule as an autocrat for three decades.
key_figures: Hüseyin Avni Pasha (minister, coup organiser — assassinated June 1876)

sources.name: Kanun-i Esasi (Ottoman Constitution), 23 December 1876
European diplomatic dispatches, 1876 (British, French, Russian archives)
François Georgeon, Abdülhamid II: Le sultan calife (2003)

events.certainty_note: Three sultans in one year is among the best-documented episodes of Ottoman political history, covered extensively in European and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Treaty of Ganghwa — Japan forces Korea to open ports
events.description: On 26 February 1876, Japan imposed the Japan–Korea Treaty of Amity (Treaty of Ganghwa) on Joseon Korea, modelled on the unequal treaties Western powers had imposed on Japan itself. The treaty followed the Ganghwa Island incident of 1875, in which a Japanese warship provoked a Korean shore battery. It declared Korea an 'independent state' (strategically undermining China's suzerain claims), opened three Korean ports to Japanese trade, and granted Japan extraterritorial rights. The treaty marked the beginning of Korea's forced integration into the modern treaty-port system and the first major step in Japanese imperial expansion on the Asian continent.

sources.name: Text of the Japan–Korea Treaty of Amity (26 February 1876)
Peter Duus, The Abacus and the Sword: The Japanese Penetration of Korea, 1895–1910 (1995)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in both Japanese and Korean versions. Diplomatic context is well documented.

events.title: Royal Titles Act — Queen Victoria proclaimed Empress of India
events.description: The Royal Titles Act, passed on 27 April 1876, authorised Queen Victoria to adopt the title 'Empress of India' (Kaiser-i-Hind), formalising British sovereign authority over the Indian subcontinent. The title was Benjamin Disraeli's initiative, intended to place Victoria on equal symbolic footing with the Russian and German emperors and to signal the permanence of British rule in India. The formal proclamation occurred at the Delhi Durbar on 1 January 1877. The act was controversial in Parliament, with critics (including Gladstone) viewing it as an un-British affectation of continental despotism.

sources.name: Royal Titles Act 1876 (39 & 40 Vict. c. 10)
Hansard, Parliamentary Debates on the Royal Titles Bill (1876)
Jane Ridley, Victoria: Queen, Matriarch, Empress (2015)

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary record and royal proclamation are fully documented.

events.title: United States Centennial Exhibition opens in Philadelphia
events.description: The International Exhibition of Arts, Manufactures and Products of the Soil and Mine — the first official World's Fair in the United States — opened on 10 May 1876 in Fairmount Park, Philadelphia, to celebrate the centennial of American independence. Nearly 10 million visitors attended over six months. The fair showcased American industrial achievement, featuring Alexander Graham Bell's telephone, the Corliss steam engine (a symbol of industrial power), and the right arm and torch of the still-incomplete Statue of Liberty. It marked the United States' arrival as an industrial power on the world stage, though its celebratory tone contrasted starkly with the ongoing depression and the violence of Reconstruction and the Indian Wars.
key_figures: Ulysses S. Grant (President, opened the exhibition)
Dom Pedro II (Emperor of Brazil, notable attendee)
George Henry Corliss (engineer, designed centrepiece engine)

sources.name: Official Catalogue of the International Exhibition, 1876
Robert W. Rydell, All the World's a Fair (1984)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records, contemporary press, and photographs.

events.title: Disputed U.S. presidential election — Hayes vs. Tilden
events.description: The presidential election of 7 November 1876 between Republican Rutherford B. Hayes and Democrat Samuel J. Tilden produced the most disputed result in American history. Tilden won the popular vote and led in the Electoral College, but results in Florida, Louisiana, South Carolina, and Oregon were contested, with both parties claiming victory. The dispute would not be resolved until the Compromise of 1877, which awarded Hayes the presidency in exchange for withdrawing federal troops from the South, effectively ending Reconstruction and abandoning Black civil rights in the former Confederacy for decades.
key_figures: Rutherford B. Hayes (Republican candidate, Ohio governor)
Samuel J. Tilden (Democratic candidate, New York governor)

sources.name: Congressional records and Electoral Commission proceedings (1876–1877)
Roy Morris Jr., Fraud of the Century: Rutherford B. Hayes, Samuel Tilden, and the Stolen Election of 1876 (2003)

events.certainty_note: Election returns and the subsequent congressional commission are fully documented in official records.

events.title: Colorado admitted as the 38th U.S. state
events.description: On 1 August 1876, Colorado was admitted to the Union as the 38th state, earning the nickname 'the Centennial State' for its admission during the nation's hundredth anniversary. Statehood followed the Colorado Enabling Act of 1875 and a territorial constitutional convention. The new state's three electoral votes would play a role in the disputed 1876 presidential election, as the state legislature (rather than popular vote) chose its electors, all of whom went to Hayes.

sources.name: Presidential Proclamation admitting Colorado (1 August 1876)
Carl Abbott, Stephen J. Leonard, and Thomas J. Noel, Colorado: A History of the Centennial State (2013)

events.certainty_note: Presidential proclamation and congressional records provide exact dating.

events.title: Northern China Famine begins
events.region: Qing China (Shanxi, Henan, Shandong, Zhili, Shaanxi)
events.description: Beginning in 1876, a catastrophic drought — linked to an exceptionally strong El Niño event — struck northern China, initiating what would become known as the Dingwu Famine (丁戊奇荒) or the Northern China Famine of 1876–1879. The drought devastated agriculture across five northern provinces. Over the next three years, between 9.5 and 13 million people died, making it one of the deadliest famines in recorded history. The Qing government's response was hampered by depleted treasuries (partly from suppressing the Taiping and Nian rebellions), corruption, and the sheer scale of the disaster. International relief efforts, including from Western missionaries, marked an early instance of transnational humanitarian response.

sources.name: Qing dynasty provincial memorials and gazettes (1876–1879)
Kathryn Edgerton-Tarpley, Tears from Iron: Cultural Responses to Famine in Nineteenth-Century China (2008)
Mike Davis, Late Victorian Holocausts: El Niño Famines and the Making of the Third World (2001)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Qing administrative records, missionary accounts, and modern climatological studies confirming the El Niño link. Death toll estimates are debated but the order of magnitude (millions) is established.

events.title: Porfirio Díaz launches the Plan de Tuxtepec — Mexican revolution
events.description: On 10 January 1876, General Porfirio Díaz proclaimed the Plan de Tuxtepec, calling for armed revolt against President Sebastián Lerdo de Tejada, whom Díaz accused of seeking unconstitutional re-election. After initial setbacks, Díaz's forces gained momentum; the decisive Battle of Tecoac on 16 November 1876 shattered the government army. Lerdo fled into exile, and Díaz entered Mexico City on 23 November. He assumed the presidency in early 1877, beginning what would become the Porfiriato — over three decades of authoritarian modernisation that ended only with the Mexican Revolution of 1910.

sources.name: Plan de Tuxtepec (10 January 1876, revised 21 March 1876)
Paul Garner, Porfirio Díaz (2001)

events.certainty_note: The Plan itself survives, and the military campaign is documented in both Mexican and U.S. diplomatic sources.

events.title: Wagner's Ring Cycle premieres at the Bayreuth Festspielhaus
events.description: On 13–17 August 1876, Richard Wagner's four-opera cycle Der Ring des Nibelungen received its first complete performance at the newly built Bayreuth Festspielhaus, a theatre Wagner designed specifically for his works. The premiere was a cultural event of pan-European significance, attended by Kaiser Wilhelm I, Emperor Dom Pedro II of Brazil, King Ludwig II of Bavaria (who had funded much of the project), and leading composers including Tchaikovsky, Grieg, and Bruckner. The Ring remains one of the most ambitious works in Western musical history, and the Bayreuth Festival continues to the present day.

sources.name: Contemporary press coverage (e.g., Neue Freie Presse, The Times of London, 1876)
Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky, letters from Bayreuth (1876)
Barry Millington, The Sorcerer of Bayreuth: Richard Wagner, His Work and His World (2012)

events.certainty_note: Massively documented event with numerous eyewitness accounts, press coverage, and correspondence.

events.title: Ethiopian Emperor Yohannes IV consolidates power — Battle of Gura
events.description: On 7–9 March 1876, Emperor Yohannes IV of Ethiopia decisively defeated an Egyptian expeditionary force at the Battle of Gura in present-day Eritrea. The Egyptians, under Ratib Pasha, had invaded Ethiopian territory as part of Khedive Ismail's expansionist programme along the Red Sea coast. The Ethiopian victory — following a smaller Egyptian defeat at Gundet in 1875 — halted Egyptian expansion into the Ethiopian highlands and cemented Yohannes IV's authority as emperor. It was one of the major African military victories against a foreign power in the 19th century, predating Adwa by two decades.

sources.name: Ethiopian royal chronicles (Amharic, later compiled)
Egyptian military records and diplomatic correspondence (1876)
Sven Rubenson, The Survival of Ethiopian Independence (1976)

events.certainty_note: Both Egyptian and Ethiopian sources attest to the battles. Exact dating of Gura is established by Egyptian military records.

events.title: Josiah Willard Gibbs publishes 'On the Equilibrium of Heterogeneous Substances'
events.description: In 1876, the American physicist and chemist J. Willard Gibbs published the first part of his monumental paper 'On the Equilibrium of Heterogeneous Substances' in the Transactions of the Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences (the second part followed in 1878). This work founded chemical thermodynamics, introducing concepts such as chemical potential, the phase rule, and Gibbs free energy that remain foundational to chemistry, physics, and materials science. Published in an obscure journal, the work was initially little known outside Europe, where it was recognised by James Clerk Maxwell and others as a landmark achievement.
key_figures: James Clerk Maxwell (early champion of Gibbs's work)

sources.name: J. Willard Gibbs, 'On the Equilibrium of Heterogeneous Substances,' Transactions of the Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences, vol. 3 (1876–1878)
Lynde Phelps Wheeler, Josiah Willard Gibbs: The History of a Great Mind (1951)

events.certainty_note: Publication dates established by journal records.

events.title: Great Sioux War begins — U.S. campaigns against Lakota and Cheyenne
events.description: The Great Sioux War of 1876 was launched after the Grant administration issued an ultimatum requiring all Lakota bands to report to reservations by 31 January 1876 or be considered hostile. The war was motivated by the desire to seize the Black Hills (Paha Sapa) following the discovery of gold in 1874. Major campaigns included General Crook's defeat at the Battle of the Rosebud (17 June), the Little Bighorn disaster (25–26 June), and the subsequent winter campaigns that gradually forced surrender. The war resulted in the U.S. seizure of the Black Hills in violation of the 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty — a violation that the U.S. Supreme Court acknowledged in 1980 (United States v. Sioux Nation).
key_figures: Philip Sheridan (Lt. Gen., commanding the campaign)

sources.name: U.S. War Department records and correspondence (1876)
Jerome A. Greene, Yellowstone Command: Colonel Nelson A. Miles and the Great Sioux War 1876–1877 (1991)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in U.S. military records, congressional debates, and Native oral histories.

events.title: Mark Twain publishes The Adventures of Tom Sawyer
events.description: In June 1876 (UK edition; the U.S. edition followed in December), Mark Twain published The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, a novel drawing on his childhood in Hannibal, Missouri. While commercially successful rather than critically groundbreaking at the time, the novel established the Mississippi River setting and the characters that would become central to Twain's masterpiece, Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1884). Tom Sawyer helped define a distinctly American literary voice and remains one of the most widely read works of American literature.

sources.name: Mark Twain, The Adventures of Tom Sawyer (1876, Chatto & Windus, London; American Publishing Company, Hartford)
Ron Powers, Mark Twain: A Life (2005)

events.certainty_note: Publication dates established by publisher records. The UK edition preceded the U.S. edition by several months.

events.title: Constantinople Conference of the Great Powers
events.description: In December 1876, the Great Powers (Britain, France, Germany, Austria-Hungary, Russia, and Italy) convened a conference in Constantinople to impose reforms on the Ottoman Empire in response to the Balkan crises — the Herzegovina uprising, the Bulgarian massacres, and the Serbian defeat. The Ottomans, under their new Sultan Abdul Hamid II, dramatically announced the adoption of a constitution on 23 December (the conference's opening day), arguing that a constitutional state did not need external tutelage. The conference failed, with the Ottomans rejecting its proposals in January 1877, directly precipitating the Russo-Turkish War.

sources.name: British Foreign Office correspondence on the Constantinople Conference (1876–1877)
M.S. Anderson, The Eastern Question, 1774–1923 (1966)

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic records from all participating powers provide detailed documentation.

events.title: Third Republic consolidates in France — constitutional crisis averted
events.description: Throughout 1876, the French Third Republic, still fragile after the upheavals of 1870–1871, saw Republicans win a decisive majority in the Chamber of Deputies elections (February–March 1876), establishing republican control of the lower house for the first time. This set the stage for the constitutional crisis of 1877 (the seize mai crisis), when President MacMahon attempted to reassert executive authority against the republican chamber. The 1876 elections were a critical step in the consolidation of republican government in France, moving it away from the monarchist and Bonapartist alternatives that had seemed viable only a few years earlier.
key_figures: Patrice de MacMahon (President of the Republic)

sources.name: Journal officiel de la République française, 1876 election results
Jean-Marie Mayeur and Madeleine Rebérioux, The Third Republic from Its Origins to the Great War, 1871–1914 (1984)

events.certainty_note: French parliamentary records and extensive press coverage document the elections and their significance.

events.title: Wild Bill Hickok killed in Deadwood, Dakota Territory
events.description: On 2 August 1876, James Butler 'Wild Bill' Hickok, the legendary frontier lawman and gunfighter, was shot and killed by Jack McCall while playing poker in a saloon in Deadwood, Dakota Territory. Hickok was reportedly holding a pair of aces and eights — thereafter known as the 'dead man's hand.' While of limited political or military consequence, Hickok's murder became one of the defining episodes of the mythology of the American West, contributing to the frontier legend that was already being constructed in dime novels and sensational journalism during this period.

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper reports (e.g., Black Hills Pioneer, August 1876)
Joseph G. Rosa, Wild Bill Hickok: The Man and His Myth (1996)

events.certainty_note: Contemporary newspaper accounts and court records from McCall's two trials establish the facts, though many details of Hickok's earlier career are embellished or legendary.

events.title: Suez Canal debt crisis — Britain acquires Egypt's shares
events.description: While Disraeli's purchase of Khedive Ismail's Suez Canal shares occurred in late 1875, the consequences unfolded decisively in 1876 as Egypt's financial crisis deepened. In April 1876, the Egyptian government suspended payment on its foreign debt, leading to the establishment of the Caisse de la Dette Publique — an Anglo-French commission that assumed control of Egyptian finances. This dual financial control was a critical step toward de facto colonial administration, culminating in the British occupation of Egypt in 1882. The crisis demonstrated how sovereign debt could become an instrument of imperial domination.

sources.name: Records of the Caisse de la Dette Publique (1876)
Roger Owen, Lord Cromer: Victorian Imperialist, Edwardian Proconsul (2004)

events.certainty_note: Financial records and diplomatic correspondence are extensive.

events.title: Robert Koch begins work on anthrax — germ theory advances
events.description: In 1876, Robert Koch demonstrated the complete life cycle of Bacillus anthracis, the bacterium causing anthrax, proving definitively that a specific microorganism caused a specific disease. Working in a makeshift laboratory in Wollstein (now Wolsztyn, Poland), Koch grew the bacterium in pure culture and reproduced the disease in laboratory animals, fulfilling what would later be formalised as Koch's postulates. His work, presented to Ferdinand Cohn in Breslau, was a foundational moment for the germ theory of disease and bacteriology as a discipline.

sources.name: Robert Koch, 'Die Ätiologie der Milzbrand-Krankheit,' Beiträge zur Biologie der Pflanzen, vol. 2 (1876)
Thomas D. Brock, Robert Koch: A Life in Medicine and Bacteriology (1988)

events.certainty_note: Koch's original publication and subsequent correspondence with Cohn provide exact documentation.

disconfirming_evidence: The Bell telephone patent priority is genuinely contested. Modern scholarship (e.g., Seth Shulman, The Telephone Gambit, 2008) has raised serious questions about whether Bell may have had access to Elisha Gray's caveat before finalising his own design, and whether the Patent Office gave Bell improper preferential treatment. The 'first words spoken' — 'Mr. Watson, come here' — appear only in Bell's notebook and Watson's much later memoir; no independent witness attested to the event at the time. The 'dead man's hand' (aces and eights) associated with Wild Bill Hickok's death is a later embellishment; the earliest accounts do not specify what cards he held. Regarding the Battle of Little Bighorn, the traditional narrative of Custer's heroic last stand has been comprehensively revised; archaeological evidence and Native testimony suggest a disorganised rout rather than a defensive stand. The death toll of the Northern China Famine varies enormously across sources — from 9.5 million to over 13 million — and recent demographic work suggests the lower end may be more defensible, though all figures involve substantial estimation. On the Bulgarian April Uprising, casualty figures (ranging from 15,000 to 30,000) remain debated, with some Bulgarian nationalist historiography inflating numbers while Ottoman sources minimise them.
historiographic_note: 1876 is richly documented across multiple regions and source traditions. However, the surviving record is heavily shaped by the perspectives of state actors and colonial powers. Ottoman history in this period has been filtered through both European diplomatic contempt and later Turkish nationalist revision. The Great Sioux War is documented overwhelmingly from the U.S. military perspective; Native oral histories were collected decades later and have only recently been given equal historiographic weight. African events (the Ethiopian-Egyptian wars) are comparatively underrepresented in English-language scholarship despite their significance. The Long Depression's impact on non-industrialised regions — particularly the famine nexus connecting China, India, Brazil, and North Africa — has been recovered primarily through the work of environmental historians like Mike Davis. Chinese famine documentation relies heavily on Qing administrative records, which were designed to report upward to the throne and carried incentives for both exaggeration and minimisation.

graph_edges.note: The telephone patent initiated the telecommunications industry that would transform global communications
Little Bighorn was the most significant battle within the broader Great Sioux War of 1876
The Bulgarian massacres and European outrage were a direct cause of Russia's declaration of war in 1877
Abdul Hamid II's rejection of the Constantinople Conference proposals led directly to war
Ganghwa Treaty's assertion of Korean 'independence' from China set up the Sino-Japanese rivalry that erupted in 1894
The disputed election was resolved by the Compromise of 1877, ending Reconstruction
The Chinese famine was part of a global El Niño-driven famine complex also affecting India, Brazil, and North Africa
Díaz's seizure of power began the Porfiriato, whose inequalities and authoritarianism produced the 1910 revolution
Ethiopian victory at Gura preserved independence and set the precedent for the decisive defeat of Italy at Adwa twenty years later
The Great Sioux War was precipitated by U.S. violation of the 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty following the Black Hills gold discovery
The Constantinople Conference was convened in response to the Bulgarian uprising and Balkan wars
Anglo-French financial control of Egypt established in 1876 was a direct precursor to British military occupation
Koch's anthrax work established the methodology he would use to identify the tuberculosis bacillus in 1882
